definition_en: to make ashamed; to embarrass
example_sentence: Meredith felt abashed by her inability to remember her lines in the school chorus of "Old McDonald Had a Farm."

definition_en: to subside; to reduce
example_sentence: George spilled a cup of hot coffee on his leg. It hurt quite a bit. Then, gradually, the agony abated.

definition_en: to step down from a position of power or responsibility
example_sentence: When King Edward VIII of England decided he would rather be married to Wallis Warfield Simpson... he turned in his crown and abdicated.

definition_en: something not typical; a deviation from the standard
definition_ko: 전형적이지 않은 것, 표준에서 벗어난 것
example_sentence: Søren's bad behavior was an aberration.

definition_en: to hate very, very much; to detest
example_sentence: Emanuel abhorred having to wake up before dawn.

definition_en: hopeless; extremely sad and servile; defeated
example_sentence: While most people would quickly recover from a stumble on stage, Mia felt abject humiliation.

definition_en: to deny oneself things; to reject; to renounce
example_sentence: Ascetics practice self-abnegation because they believe it will bring them closer to spiritual purity.

definition_en: unsuccessful
example_sentence: Marie and Elizabeth made an abortive effort to bake a birthday cake; that is, their effort did not result in a birthday cake.

definition_en: to shorten; to condense
example_sentence: The thoughtful editor abridged the massive book by removing the boring parts.

definition_en: total; unlimited
example_sentence: An absolute ruler is one who is ruled by no one else.

definition_en: to forgive or free from blame; to free from sin; to free from an obligation
example_sentence: The priest absolved the sinner who had come to church to confess.

definition_en: abstaining; voluntarily not doing something, especially something pleasant that is bad for you or has a bad reputation
example_sentence: Beulah used to be smoker; now she's abstinent.

definition_en: theoretical; impersonal
example_sentence: He liked oysters in the abstract, but when he actually tried one he became nauseated.

definition_en: hard to understand
example_sentence: The professor's article, on the meaning of meaning, was abstruse.

definition_en: extremely hopeless or wretched; bottomless
example_sentence: The nation's debt crisis was abysmal; there seemed to be no possible solution.

definition_en: an award; an honor
example_sentence: The first break-dancing troupe to perform in Carnegie Hall, the Teflon Toughs, received accolades from the critics as well as from the fans.

definition_en: to approach and speak to someone aggressively
example_sentence: Amanda karate-chopped the stranger who accosted her in the street and was embarrassed to find he was an old, blind man.

definition_en: sour; severe; like acid in temper, mood, or tone
definition_ko: 신맛이 나는, 태도나 기분이 거친, 신랄한
example_sentence: Barry sat silently as his friends read the teacher's acerbic comments on his paper.

definition_en: to comply passively; to accept; to assent; to agree
example_sentence: The pirates asked Pete to walk the plank; he took one look at their swords and then acquiesced.
etymology_hint: Latin roots meaning to rest or to be quiet

definition_en: harshly pungent; bitter
definition_ko: 콕 쏘는 듯한, 쓴, 톡 쏘는
example_sentence: The cheese we had at the party had an acrid taste; it was harsh and unpleasant.

definition_en: full of spite; bitter; nasty
example_sentence: Relations between the competing candidates were so acrimonious that each refused to acknowledge the presence of the other.

definition_en: keenness of judgment; mental sharpness
example_sentence: A woman who knows how to turn one dollar into a million overnight might be said to have a lot of business acumen.

definition_en: sharp; shrewd
example_sentence: If your eyesight is acute, you can see things that other people can't.

definition_en: stubborn; unyielding; completely inflexible
definition_ko: 완고한, 타협하지 않는, 절대 굽히지 않는
example_sentence: Candice was adamant: she would never go out with Paul again.

definition_en: to speak to; to direct one's attention to
example_sentence: Ernie addressed the problem of addressing the convention by sitting down and writing his speech.

definition_en: follower; supporter; believer
example_sentence: The king's adherents threw a big birthday party for him, just to show how much they liked him.

definition_en: to scold gently; to warn
example_sentence: The boys' father admonished them not to eat the pie he had just baked.

definition_en: skillful; dexterous; clever; shrewd; socially at ease
example_sentence: Julio was an adroit salesperson: his highly skilled pitch... nearly always resulted in a sale.

definition_en: wild or excessive admiration; flattery
example_sentence: The boss thrived on the adulation of his scheming secretary.

definition_en: to contaminate; to make impure
example_sentence: We discovered that the town's drinking water had radioactive waste in it; we discovered, in other words, that it had been adulterated.

definition_en: unfavorable; antagonistic
example_sentence: We had to play our soccer match under adverse conditions: It was snowing, and only three members of our team had bothered to show up.

definition_en: having to do with artistic beauty; artistic
example_sentence: Our art professor had a highly developed aesthetic sense; he found things to admire in paintings that, to us, looked like garbage.

definition_en: easy to talk to; friendly
example_sentence: Susan was an affable girl; she could strike up a pleasant conversation with almost anyone.

definition_en: unnatural or artificial behavior, usually intended to impress
example_sentence: Becky's English accent is an affectation. She spent only a week in England, and that was several years ago.

definition_en: sympathy; attraction; kinship; similarity
example_sentence: Ducks have an affinity for water; that is, they like to be in it.

definition_en: rich; prosperous
example_sentence: A person can be affluent; all it takes is money.

definition_en: program; the things to be done
example_sentence: What's on the agenda for the board meeting? A little gossip, then lunch.

definition_en: sum total; a collection of separate things mixed together
example_sentence: Chili is an aggregate of meat and beans.

definition_en: one who believes that the existence of a god can be neither proven nor disproven
example_sentence: An atheist himself, Jon concluded from Jorge's spiritual skepticism that they shared similar beliefs. In fact, Jorge's reluctance to affirm or discredit a god's existence reflects his agnosticism.

confusions.difference: agnostic=신이 있는지 없는지 모른다는 사람/atheist=신이 없다고 믿는 무신론자

definition_en: relating to land; relating to the management or farming of land
example_sentence: Politics in this country often pit the rural, agrarian interests against the urban interests.

definition_en: cheerful eagerness or readiness to respond
example_sentence: David could hardly wait for his parents to leave; he carried their luggage out to the car with great alacrity.

definition_en: to assert without proof
example_sentence: The police have alleged that Mary committed the crime, but a jury hasn't made a decision yet.

definition_en: to relieve, usually temporarily or incompletely; to make bearable; to lessen
example_sentence: Visiting the charming pet cemetery alleviated the woman's grief over the death of her canary.

definition_en: to distribute; to assign; to allot
example_sentence: The office manager had allocated just seven paper clips for our entire department.

definition_en: a combination of two or more things, usually metals
example_sentence: Brass is an alloy of copper and zinc, meaning you make brass by combining copper and zinc.

definition_en: an indirect reference (often to a literary work); a hint
example_sentence: When Ralph said, "I sometimes wonder whether to be or not to be," he was alluding to a famous line in Hamlet.

definition_en: uninvolved; standing off; keeping one's distance
example_sentence: Al, on the roof, felt very aloof.

definition_en: selflessness; generosity; devotion to the interests of others
example_sentence: The private foundation depended on the altruism of the extremely rich old man.

definition_en: atmosphere; mood; feeling
example_sentence: By decorating their house with plastic beach balls and Popsicle sticks, the Cramers created a playful ambience that delighted young children.

definition_en: unclear in meaning; confusing; capable of being interpreted in different ways
example_sentence: We listened to the weather report, but the forecast was ambiguous; we couldn't tell whether the day was going to be rainy or sunny.

definition_en: undecided; having opposed feelings simultaneously
example_sentence: Susan felt ambivalent about Alec as a boyfriend. Her frequent desire to break up with him reflected this ambivalence.

definition_en: to make better or more tolerable
example_sentence: The mood of the prisoners was ameliorated when the warden gave them extra free time outside.

definition_en: obedient; willing to give in to the wishes of another; agreeable
example_sentence: I suggested that Brad pay for my lunch as well as for his own; to my surprise, he was amenable.

definition_en: pleasantness; attractive or comfortable feature
example_sentence: The amenities at the local club include a swimming pool, a golf course, and a tennis court.

definition_en: friendly; agreeable
example_sentence: Our amiable guide made us feel right at home in what would otherwise have been a cold and forbidding museum.

definition_en: an official pardon for a group of people who have violated a law or policy
example_sentence: When a state government declares a tax amnesty, it is saying that if people pay the taxes they owe, the government will officially "forget" that they broke the law...

definition_en: lacking a sense of right and wrong; neither good nor bad, neither moral nor immoral; without moral feelings
example_sentence: Very young children are amoral; when they cry, they aren't being bad or good-they're merely doing what they have to do.

definition_en: feeling loving, especially in a sexual sense; in love; relating to love
example_sentence: The amorous couple made quite a scene at the movie.

definition_en: shapeless; without a regular or stable shape; bloblike
example_sentence: Ed's teacher said that his term paper was amorphous; it was as shapeless and disorganized as a cloud.

definition_en: something out of place in time or history; an incongruity
example_sentence: In this day of impersonal hospitals, a doctor who remembers your name seems like an anachronism.

definition_en: a comparison of one thing to another; similarity
example_sentence: To say having an allergy feels like being bitten by an alligator would be to make or draw an analogy between an allergy and an alligator bite.

definition_en: absence of government or control; lawlessness; disorder
example_sentence: The country fell into a state of anarchy after the rebels kidnapped the president and locked the legislature inside the Capitol.

confusions.difference: anarchy=지도자가 없는 무정부 상태/monarchy=왕이나 여왕 1인이 다스리는 군주제

definition_en: a short account of a humorous or revealing incident
example_sentence: The old lady kept the motorcycle gang thoroughly amused with anecdote after anecdote about her cute little dog.

definition_en: agonizing physical or mental pain
example_sentence: Theresa had been a nurse in the emergency room for twenty years, but she had never gotten used to the anguish of accident victims.

definition_en: resentment; hostility; ill will
example_sentence: The rivals for the state championship felt great animosity toward each other.

definition_en: an aberration; an irregularity; a deviation
example_sentence: A snowy winter day is not an anomaly, but a snowy July day is.

definition_en: someone or something that went before; something that provides a model for something that came after it
example_sentence: Your parents and grandparents could be said to be your antecedents; they came before you.

definition_en: firm dislike
example_sentence: I feel antipathy toward bananas wrapped in ham.

definition_en: the direct opposite
example_sentence: Erin is the antithesis of Aaron: Erin is bright and beautiful; Aaron is dull and plain.

definition_en: the former policy of racial segregation and oppression in the Republic of South Africa
example_sentence: Critics have sometimes accused American public schools of practicing educational apartheid by providing substandard schooling for nonwhites.

definition_en: lack of interest; lack of feeling
example_sentence: The members of the student council accused the senior class of apathy because none of the seniors had bothered to sign up for the big fundraiser.

definition_en: a brief, often witty saying; a proverb
example_sentence: Benjamin Franklin was fond of aphorisms.

definition_en: a prophetic revelation, especially one concerning the end of the world
definition_ko: 세상의 종말에 대한 예언적 계시, 대참사
example_sentence: To make such predictions, or to be deeply pessimistic, is to be apocalyptic.

definition_en: of dubious authenticity; fictitious; spurious
example_sentence: Brandi's blog discredited the apocryphal report of Martians in Congress.

definition_en: elevation to divine status; the perfect example of something
example_sentence: Some people think that the Corvette is the apotheosis of American car making.

definition_en: to soothe; to pacify by giving in to
example_sentence: Jaleel appeased his angry mother by promising to make his bed every morning without fail until the end of time.

definition_en: to increase in value
example_sentence: The Browns bought their house twenty years ago for a hundred thousand dollars, but it has appreciated considerably since then...

definition_en: worried; anxious
example_sentence: The apprehensive child clung to his father's leg as the two of them walked into the main circus tent...

definition_en: approval; praise
example_sentence: The crowd expressed its approbation of the team's performance by gleefully covering the field with toilet paper.

definition_en: to take without permission; to set aside for a particular use
definition_ko: 무단으로 사용하다, 허락 없이 가지다, 특정 용도로 할당하다
example_sentence: Nick appropriated my lunch; he grabbed it out of my hands and ate it.

definition_en: capacity for learning; natural ability
example_sentence: Some rare students have a marked aptitude for taking the SAT.

definition_en: one who decides; a judge
example_sentence: An arbiter of fashion determines what other people will wear by wearing it herself.

definition_en: random; capricious
example_sentence: The grades Mr. Simone gave his English students appeared to be arbitrary...

definition_en: mysterious; known only to a select few
example_sentence: The rites of the secret cult were arcane; no one outside the cult knew what they were.

definition_en: extremely old; ancient; outdated
example_sentence: The tribe's traditions are archaic. They have been in force for thousands of years.

definition_en: an original model or pattern
example_sentence: Plato is the archetype of all philosophers.

definition_en: passionate; enthusiastic
example_sentence: Larry's ardent wooing finally got on Cynthia's nerves, and she told him to get lost.

definition_en: hard; difficult
example_sentence: Climbing the mountain was arduous.

definition_en: of noble birth; snobbish
example_sentence: Prince Charles is aristocratic.

definition_en: crafty; wily; sly
example_sentence: After dinner, the artful counselor told the campers that there was a madman loose in the woods...

definition_en: a clever trick; cunning
example_sentence: The Trojan Horse was an artifice designed to get the soldiers inside the walls.

definition_en: supremacy; domination
example_sentence: Handheld gadgets have been in ascendancy for the past few years.

definition_en: hermitlike; practicing self-denial
example_sentence: The college student's apartment, which contained no furniture except a single tattered mattress, was uncomfortably ascetic.

definition_en: hardworking; busy; quite diligent
example_sentence: Wendell was the only assiduous student in the entire math class; all the other students tried to copy their homework from him.

definition_en: to take in; to absorb; to learn thoroughly
example_sentence: Margaret didn't have any friends when she first went to the new school, but she was gradually assimilated...

definition_en: to soothe; to pacify; to ease the pain of; to relieve
example_sentence: Beth was extremely angry, but I assuaged her by promising to leave the house and never return.

definition_en: shrewd; keen in judgment
example_sentence: Morris was an astute judge of character...

definition_en: one who does not believe in the existence of any god or divine being
example_sentence: Hadley had always imagined a big religious wedding, but Emma, a lifelong atheist, preferred a Vegas elopement.

confusions.difference: atheist=신이 없다고 확신/agnostic=신의 존재를 증명할 수 없다고 봄

definition_en: gradual wearing away, weakening, or loss; a natural or expected decrease in numbers or size
example_sentence: Mr. Gregory did not have the heart to fire his workers... He altruistically preferred to lose workers through attrition...

definition_en: boldness; reckless daring; impertinence
example_sentence: Edgar's soaring leap off the top of the building was an act of great audacity.

definition_en: to make bigger; to add to; to increase
example_sentence: The army augmented its attack by sending in a few thousand more soldiers.

definition_en: favorable; promising; pointing to a good result
example_sentence: A clear sky in the morning is an auspicious sign on the day of a picnic.

definition_en: unadorned; stern; forbidding; without excess
example_sentence: The Smiths' house was austere: there was no furniture in it, and there was nothing hanging on the walls.

definition_en: ruling with absolute authority; extremely bossy
example_sentence: The ruthless dictator's autocratic reign ended when the rebels blew up his palace with plastic explosives.

definition_en: acting independently
example_sentence: The West Coast office of the law firm was quite autonomous; it never asked the East Coast office for permission before it did anything.

definition_en: greed; excessive love of riches
example_sentence: The rich man's avarice was annoying to everyone who wanted to lay hands on his money.

definition_en: to claim; to declare boldly; to admit
example_sentence: At the age of twenty-five, Louis finally avowed that he couldn't stand his mother's apple pie.

definition_en: like an uncle, especially a nice uncle
example_sentence: Professor Zia often gave us avuncular advice; he took a real interest in our education...

definition_en: off course; twisted to one side
example_sentence: The hunter's bullet went awry. Instead of hitting the bear, it hit his truck.

definition_en: a self-evident rule or truth; a widely accepted saying
example_sentence: "Everything that is living dies" is an axiom.

definition_en: unoriginal; ordinary
example_sentence: The dinner conversation was so banal that Amanda fell asleep in her dessert dish.

definition_en: poison; torment; cause of harm
example_sentence: To say that someone is the bane of your existence is to say that that person poisons your enjoyment of life.

definition_en: stronghold; fortress; fortified place
example_sentence: The robbers terrorized the village for several weeks and then escaped to their bastion high in the treacherous mountains.

definition_en: to give birth to; to create; to lead to; to cause
example_sentence: Those who lie should be creative and have good memories, because one lie often begets another lie, which begets another.

definition_en: to go over repeatedly or to an absurd extent
example_sentence: For more than an hour, the boring speaker belabored her point about the challenge of foreign competition.

definition_en: to surround; to besiege; to harass
example_sentence: No one could leave the beleaguered city; the attacking army had closed off all the exits.

definition_en: to give a false impression of; to contradict
example_sentence: Melvin's smile belied the grief he was feeling; despite his happy expression he was terribly sad inside.

definition_en: to make to seem little; to put someone down
example_sentence: The chairman's belittling comments made everyone feel small.

definition_en: combative; quarrelsome; waging war
example_sentence: Al was so belligerent that the party had the feel of a boxing match.

definition_en: confused; bewildered
example_sentence: Ralph was bemused when all lights and appliances in his house began switching on and off for no apparent reason.

definition_en: one who provides help, especially in the form of a gift or donation
example_sentence: If your next-door neighbor dies, he is your benefactor.

definition_en: generous; kind; doing good deeds
example_sentence: Giving money to the poor is a benevolent act.

definition_en: gentle; not harmful; kind; mild
example_sentence: Charlie was worried that he had cancer, but the lump on his leg turned out to be benign.

definition_en: something left to someone in a will
example_sentence: If your next-door neighbor leaves you all his millions in a will, the money is a bequest from him to you.

definition_en: deprived or left desolate, especially through death
example_sentence: The new widow was still bereaved when we saw her.

definition_en: to harass; to surround
example_sentence: Problems beset the expedition almost from the beginning, and the mountain climbers soon returned to their base camp.

definition_en: irreverence; an insult to something held sacred; profanity
example_sentence: In the strictest sense, to commit blasphemy is to say nasty, insulting things about God.

definition_en: unpleasantly or offensively noisy; glaring
example_sentence: David was blatantly critical of our efforts; that is, he was noisy and obnoxious in making his criticisms.

definition_en: a disease in plants; anything that injures or destroys
example_sentence: An early frost proved a blight to the citrus crops last year, so we had no orange juice for breakfast.

definition_en: carefree; cheerful
example_sentence: The children were playing blithely next to the hazardous-waste dump, unaware that they were doing something dangerous.

definition_en: middle class, usually in a pejorative sense; boringly conventional
example_sentence: A hip, young city dweller might reject life in the suburbs as being too bourgeois.

definition_en: cow related; cowlike
example_sentence: Cows are bovine, obviously. Eating grass is a bovine concern.

definition_en: briefness
example_sentence: The audience was deeply grateful for the brevity of the after-dinner speaker's remarks.

definition_en: to open up a subject for discussion, often a delicate subject
example_sentence: Henrietta was proud of her new dress, so no one knew how to broach the subject with her of how silly grandmothers look in leather.

definition_en: charmingly rural; rustic; countrylike
example_sentence: The changing of the autumn leaves, old stone walls, distant views, and horses grazing in green meadows are examples of bucolic splendor.

definition_en: a system of government administration consisting of numerous bureaus or offices; any large administrative system characterized by inefficiency, lots of rules, and red tape
example_sentence: The Department of Motor Vehicles is a bureaucracy.

definition_en: to expand; to flourish
example_sentence: The burgeoning weeds in our yard soon overwhelmed the grass.

definition_en: a ludicrous, mocking, lewd imitation
example_sentence: Vaudeville actors frequently performed burlesque works on the stage.

definition_en: harsh-sounding mixture of words, voices, or sounds
example_sentence: The parade's two marching bands played simultaneously; the resulting cacophony drove many spectators to tears.

definition_en: rhythm; the rise and fall of sounds
example_sentence: We wished the tone of Irwin's words would have a more pleasing cadence, but he spoke in a dull monotone.

definition_en: to persuade someone to do something he or she doesn't want to do
example_sentence: I didn't want to give the speech, but Enrique cajoled me into doing it by telling me what a good speaker I am.

definition_en: immature
example_sentence: The patient was alarmed by the callowness of the medical staff. The doctors looked too young to have graduated from high school.

definition_en: truthfulness; sincere honesty
example_sentence: My best friend exhibited candor when she told me that for many years now she has believed me to be a jerk.

definition_en: an economic system in which businesses are owned by private citizens (not by the government) and in which the resulting products and services are sold with relatively little government control
example_sentence: The American economy is capitalist.

definition_en: to surrender; to give up or give in
example_sentence: On the twentieth day of the strike, the workers capitulated and went back to work without a new contract.

definition_en: unpredictable; likely to change at any moment
example_sentence: Arjun was capricious. One minute he said his favorite car was a Volkswagen; the next minute he said it was a Toyota.

definition_en: a portrait or description that is purposely distorted or exaggerated, often to prove some point about its subject
example_sentence: Khoa sat for a caricature at the end of the marathon, but wasn't pleased with the result: the portrait exaggerated his already dominant acne.

definition_en: to criticize severely; to chastise
example_sentence: Jose's mother-in-law castigated him for forgetting to pick her up at the airport.

definition_en: in chemistry, something that changes the rate of a chemical reaction without itself being changed; anyone or anything that makes something happen without being directly involved in it
example_sentence: When the mad scientist dropped a few grains of the catalyst into his test tube, the bubbling liquid began to boil furiously.

definition_en: unconditional; absolute
example_sentence: I categorically refuse to do anything whatsoever at any time, in any place, with anyone.

definition_en: purification that brings emotional relief or renewal
example_sentence: To someone with psychological problems, talking to a psychiatrist can lead to a catharsis.

definition_en: universal; embracing everything
example_sentence: Da Vinci was a catholic genius who excelled at everything he did.

definition_en: like acid; corrosive
example_sentence: The teacher's caustic criticism of Sally's term paper left her crestfallen.

definition_en: abstinence from sex
example_sentence: People who practice celibacy don't practice sex.

definition_en: to condemn severely for doing something bad
example_sentence: The Senate sometimes censures senators for breaking laws or engaging in behavior unbecoming to an elected official.

definition_en: brainy; intellectually refined
example_sentence: Sebastian was too cerebral to be a baseball announcer; he kept talking about the existentialism of the outfield.

definition_en: humiliation; embarrassed disappointment
example_sentence: Much to my chagrin, I began to giggle during the eulogy at the funeral.

definition_en: a magical-seeming ability to attract followers or inspire loyalty
example_sentence: The glamorous presidential candidate had a lot of charisma; voters didn't seem to support her so much as be entranced by her.

definition_en: fraud; quack; con man
example_sentence: Buck was selling what he claimed was a cure for cancer, but he was just a charlatan.

definition_en: a deep, gaping hole; a gorge
example_sentence: The bad guys were gaining, so the hero grabbed the heroine and swung across the chasm on a slender vine.

definition_en: to inflict punishment on; to discipline
example_sentence: Mother chastised us for firing our bottle rockets too close to the house.

definition_en: trickery; deceitfulness; artifice, especially legal or political
example_sentence: Political news would be dull were it not for the chicanery of our elected officials.

definition_en: an illusion; a foolish fancy
example_sentence: Jie's dream of becoming a movie star was just a chimera.

definition_en: hot-tempered; quick to anger
example_sentence: The choleric watchdog would sink his teeth into anyone who came within biting distance of his doghouse.

definition_en: constant; lasting a long time; inveterate
example_sentence: DJ's chronic back pains often kept her from soccer practice.

definition_en: a record of events in order of time; a history
example_sentence: Sally's diary provided her mother with a detailed chronicle of her daughter's extracurricular activities.

definition_en: roundabout; not following a direct path
example_sentence: The circuitous bus route between the two cities went here, there, and everywhere, and it took an extremely long time to get anywhere.

definition_en: an indirect expression; use of wordy or evasive language
example_sentence: The lawyer's circumlocution left everyone in the courtroom wondering what had been said.

definition_en: to draw a line around; to set the limits; to define; to restrict
example_sentence: The Constitution clearly circumscribes the restrictions that can be placed on our personal freedoms.

definition_en: cautious
example_sentence: As a public speaker, Nick was extremely circumspect; he always took great care not to say the wrong thing or give offense.
etymology_hint: Greek roots meaning around and look

definition_en: to frustrate as though by surrounding
example_sentence: Our hopes for an early end of the meeting were circumvented by the chairperson's refusal to deal with the items on the agenda.

definition_en: polite; civilized; courteous
example_sentence: Our dinner guests conducted themselves civilly when we told them we weren't going to serve them dinner after all.

definition_en: mercy; forgiveness; mildness
example_sentence: The governor committed an act of clemency when he released all the convicts from the state penitentiary.

definition_en: an overused saying or idea
example_sentence: The expression "you can't judge a book by its cover" is a cliché; it's been used so many times that it's become stale.

definition_en: an exclusive group bound together by some shared quality or interest
example_sentence: The high school newspaper staff was a real clique; they all hung out together and wouldn't talk to anyone else.

definition_en: to come together as one; to fuse; to unite
example_sentence: When the dough coalesced into a big blob, we began to wonder whether the cookies would be edible.

definition_en: to force someone to do or not to do something
example_sentence: Darth Vader tried to flatter, bribe, and even coerce, but he was never able to make Han Solo reveal the hidden rebel base.

definition_en: powerfully convincing
example_sentence: Shaft was cogent in explaining why he needed the confidential files, so we gave them to him.

definition_en: dealing with how we know the world around us through our senses; mental
example_sentence: Scientists study the cognitive apparatus of human beings to pattern how computers should gather information about the world.

definition_en: aware; conscious
example_sentence: Al was cognizant of the dangers of sword swallowing, but he tried it anyway and hurt himself quite badly.

definition_en: holding together; making sense
example_sentence: After puzzling over Grace's disorganized Holy Roman Empire essay for almost an hour, Ms. Fabricius needed only twenty minutes to read Arjun's coherent paper.

definition_en: conversational; informal in language
example_sentence: A writer with a colloquial style is a writer who uses ordinary words and whose writing seems as informal as common speech.

definition_en: conspiracy; secret cooperation
example_sentence: The increase in oil prices was the result of collusion by the oil-producing nations.

definition_en: equal; proportionate
example_sentence: Ryan's salary is commensurate with his ability; like his ability, his salary is small.

definition_en: forceful; causing to yield
example_sentence: A compelling argument for buying a security system is one that makes you go out and buy a security system.

definition_en: a summary; an abridgment
example_sentence: A yearbook often contains a compendium of the offenses, achievements, and future plans of the members of the senior class.

definition_en: self-satisfied; overly pleased with oneself; contented to a fault

definition_en: the pursuit of pleasure as a way of life

definition_en: leadership, especially of one nation over another

definition_en: any belief that is strongly opposed to established beliefs

definition_en: impervious to external influence; airtight

definition_en: golden age; prime

definition_en: a break or interruption, often from work

definition_en: an organization based on rank or degree; pecking order

definition_en: overly dramatic; theatrical

definition_en: a sermon

definition_en: uniform; made entirely of one thing

definition_en: thrifty management of resources; livestock farming

definition_en: an exaggeration used as a figure of speech; exaggeration

definition_en: uncertain; unproven

definition_en: one who attacks popular beliefs or institutions

definition_en: a system of social or political ideas

definition_en: a peculiarity; an eccentricity

definition_en: charming in a rustic way; naturally peaceful

definition_en: deep disgrace

definition_en: illegal; not permitted

definition_en: to move permanently to a new country

definition_en: just about to happen

definition_en: unchangeable

definition_en: fair; not favoring one side or the other; unbiased

definition_en: flawless; entirely without sin

definition_en: like an emperor or an empire

definition_en: bossy and, usually, arrogant

definition_en: not allowing anything to pass through; impenetrable

definition_en: impulsive; extremely impatient

definition_en: to carry out

definition_en: powerless; helpless; unable to perform

definition_en: to attack, especially to attack the truth or integrity of something

definition_en: silly; senseless

definition_en: to begin officially; to induct formally into office

definition_en: brilliant; giving off heat or light

definition_en: a chant; the repetition of statements or phrases in a way reminiscent of a chant

definition_en: to make very angry

definition_en: unceasing

definition_en: beginning; emerging

definition_en: not harmonious; not consistent; not appropriate; not fitting in

definition_en: incapable of being reformed

definition_en: not caring one way or the other; apathetic; mediocre

definition_en: native; originating in that area

definition_en: poor

definition_en: angry, especially as a result of something unjust or unworthy; insulted

definition_en: lazy

definition_en: lenient; yielding to desire
definition_ko: 관대한, 하고 싶은 대로 다 하게 냅두는

definition_en: incapable of being expressed or described

definition_en: clumsy; incompetent; gauche

definition_en: inactive; sluggish; not reacting chemically

definition_en: relentless; inevitable; unavoidable

definition_en: foolish; foolishly passionate or attracted; made foolish; foolishly in love

definition_en: to conclude; to deduce

definition_en: very, very, very small; infinitely small

definition_en: frank; without deception; simple; artless; charmingly naive

definition_en: part of the essential nature of something; intrinsic

definition_en: existing since birth; inborn; inherent

definition_en: hard or impossible to satisfy; greedy; avaricious

definition_en: treacherous; sneaky

definition_en: to hint; to creep in

definition_en: dull; bland; banal

definition_en: arrogant; insulting

definition_en: to provoke; to stir up

definition_en: like an island; isolated

definition_en: a rebel; someone who revolts against a government

definition_en: essential

definition_en: to combine two or more things into a whole

definition_en: uncontrollable; stubborn; disobedient

definition_en: uncompromising; stubborn

definition_en: part of the essential nature of something; inherent

definition_en: tending to think about oneself; examining one's feelings

definition_en: to flood; to cover completely with water; to overwhelm

definition_en: insulting or abusive speech

definition_en: exercising sound judgment

definition_en: to place side by side

definition_en: having to do with motion; lively; active

definition_en: a maze; something like a maze

definition_en: to mourn

definition_en: to praise; to applaud; to extol; to celebrate

definition_en: something handed down from the past; a bequest

definition_en: sluggishness; laziness; drowsiness; indifference

definition_en: lightness; frivolity; unseriousness

definition_en: talking a lot or too much

definition_en: forgiving; unresentful; noble in spirit; generous

definition_en: warlike; having to do with combat

definition_en: a fundamental principle; an old saying

definition_en: the belief that there are no values or morals in the universe

definition_en: fatherly; fatherlike

definition_en: the science of diseases

definition_en: to spread or seep through; to penetrate

definition_en: deadly; extremely evil
example_sentence: The drug dealers conducted their pernicious business on every street corner in the city.

definition_en: a privilege that goes along with a job; a "perk"
example_sentence: Free access to a photocopier is a perquisite of most office jobs.

definition_en: relevant; dealing with the matter at hand
example_sentence: The suspect said that he was just borrowing the jewelry for a costume ball. The cop said he did not think that was pertinent.

definition_en: to disturb greatly
example_sentence: Rudolph's mother was perturbed by his aberrant behavior at the dinner table.

definition_en: to read carefully
example_sentence: The lawyer perused the contract for many hours, looking for a loophole that would enable his client to back out of the deal.

definition_en: to spread throughout
example_sentence: A terrible smell pervaded the apartment building after the sewer main exploded.

definition_en: rude; cranky; ill-tempered
example_sentence: Gloria became petulant when we suggested that she leave her pet cheetah at home.

definition_en: love of humankind, especially by doing good deeds
example_sentence: His gift of one billion dollars to the local orphanage was the finest act of philanthropy I've ever seen.

definition_en: a smugly ignorant person with no appreciation of intellectual or artistic matters
example_sentence: The novelist dismissed her critics as philistines, saying they wouldn't recognize a good book if it crawled up and bit them on the nose.

definition_en: reverent or devout; outwardly (and sometimes falsely) reverent or devout; hypocritical
example_sentence: The adulterous minister's sermon on marital fidelity was filled with pious disregard for his own sins.

definition_en: crucial
example_sentence: Sofia's contribution was pivotal; without it, we would have failed.

definition_en: to pacify; to appease; to soothe
example_sentence: The tribe placated the angry volcano by tossing a few teenagers into the raging crater.

definition_en: expressing sadness or sorrow
example_sentence: The lead singer's plaintive love song expressed his sorrow at being abandoned by his girlfriend for the lead guitarist.

definition_en: a dull or trite remark; a cliché
example_sentence: The principal thinks he is a great orator, but his loud, boring speech was full of platitudes.

definition_en: common; vulgar; low-class
example_sentence: Sarah refused to eat frozen dinners, saying they were too plebeian for her discriminating palate.

definition_en: an excess
example_sentence: We ate a plethora of candy on Halloween and a plethora of turkey on Thanksgiving.

definition_en: painfully emotional; extremely moving; sharp or astute
example_sentence: All the reporters stopped taking notes as they watched the old woman's poignant reunion with her daughter, whom she hadn't seen in 45 years.

definition_en: to break up into opposing factions or groupings
example_sentence: The increasingly acrimonious debate between the two candidates polarized the political party.

definition_en: a powerful argument often made to attack or refute a controversial issue
example_sentence: The book was a convincing polemic that revealed the fraud at the heart of the large corporation.

definition_en: so large as to be clumsy; massive; dull
example_sentence: The wedding cake was a ponderous blob of icing and jelly beans.

definition_en: an omen; a sign of something coming in the future
example_sentence: The distant rumbling we heard this morning was a portent of the thunderstorm that hit our area this afternoon.

definition_en: something accepted as true without proof; an axiom
example_sentence: We might be able to prove a postulate if we had the time, but not now.

definition_en: practical; down-to-earth; based on experience rather than theory
example_sentence: Erecting a gigantic dome of gold over our house would have been the ideal solution... but the small size of our bank account forced us to be pragmatic.

definition_en: an earlier example or model of something
example_sentence: Last year's million-dollar prom set a precedent that the current student council hopes will not be followed in the future.

definition_en: a rule to live by; a principle establishing a certain kind of action or behavior; a maxim
example_sentence: "Love thy neighbor" is a precept we have sometimes found difficult to follow.

definition_en: to cause to happen abruptly
example_sentence: A panic among investors precipitated last Monday's crisis in the stock market.

definition_en: steep
example_sentence: A mountain can be precipitous, meaning either that it is steep or that it comprises lots of steep cliffs.

definition_en: to prevent something from ever happening
example_sentence: Ann feared that her abysmal academic career might preclude her becoming a brain surgeon.

definition_en: forerunner; something that goes before and anticipates or paves the way for whatever it is that follows
example_sentence: A sore throat is often the precursor of a cold.

definition_en: a natural preference for something
example_sentence: The impatient judge had a predilection for well-prepared lawyers who said what they meant and didn't waste her time.

definition_en: better than anyone else; outstanding; supreme
example_sentence: The Nobel Prize-winning physicist was preeminent in his field but he was still a lousy teacher.

definition_en: to seize something by prior right
example_sentence: When television show A preempts television show B, television show A is shown at the time usually reserved for television show B.

definition_en: an assumption; the basis for a conclusion
example_sentence: In deciding to eat all the ice cream in the freezer, my premise was that if I didn't do it, you would.

definition_en: to preoccupy; to influence beforehand or prejudice; to make a good impression on beforehand
definition_ko: 마음을 사로잡다, 미리 영향을 주다, 호감을 주다
example_sentence: My dream of producing energy from old chewing-gum wrappers prepossessed me, and I lost my job, my home, my wife, and my children.

definition_en: a right or privilege connected exclusively with a position, a person, a class, a nation, or some other group or classification
example_sentence: Giving traffic tickets to people he didn't like was one of the prerogatives of Junior's job as a policeman.

definition_en: to triumph; to overcome rivals; (with on, upon, or with) to persuade
example_sentence: The prosecutor prevailed in the murder trial; the defendant was found guilty.

definition_en: original; unspoiled; pure
example_sentence: An antique in pristine condition is one that hasn't been tampered with over the years.

definition_en: wastefully extravagant
example_sentence: The prodigal gambler soon found that she couldn't afford even a two-dollar bet.

definition_en: extraordinary; enormous
example_sentence: To fill the Grand Canyon with ping-pong balls would be a prodigious undertaking.

definition_en: an extremely talented child; an extraordinary accomplishment or occurrence
example_sentence: The three-year-old prodigy could play all of Beethoven and most of Brahms on her harmonica.

definition_en: not having to do with religion; irreverent; blasphemous
example_sentence: Worshiping the almighty dollar is profane.

definition_en: to declare; to declare falsely or pretend
example_sentence: Jason professed to have taught himself calculus.

definition_en: thoroughly competent; skillful; good (at something)
example_sentence: Lillian was a proficient cabinetmaker. She could make a cabinet that would make you sit back and say, "Now, there's a cabinet."

definition_en: extravagantly wasteful and, usually, wildly immoral
example_sentence: The fraternity members were a profligate bunch; they held all-night parties on weeknights and nearly burned down their fraternity house.

definition_en: deep (in several senses)
example_sentence: To say something profound is to say something deeply intelligent or discerning.

definition_en: flowing; extravagant
example_sentence: When we gave Marian our house, our car, and all our clothes, her gratitude was profuse.

definition_en: the industrial working class
example_sentence: The proletariat is the laboring class-blue-collar workers or people who roll up their shirtsleeves to do an honest day's work.

definition_en: to spread or grow rapidly
example_sentence: Honey bees proliferated when we filled our yard with flowering plants.

definition_en: abundantly productive; fruitful or fertile
example_sentence: A prolific writer is a writer who writes a lot of books.

definition_en: to proclaim; to publicly or formally declare something
example_sentence: The principal promulgated a new dress code over the loud-speaker system.

definition_en: a natural inclination or tendency; a predilection
example_sentence: Jessie has a propensity for saying stupid things: every time she opens her mouth, something stupid comes out.

definition_en: marked by favorable signs or conditions
example_sentence: Rush hour is not a propitious time to drive into the city.

definition_en: an advocate; a supporter of a position
example_sentence: The proponents of a tax increase will probably not be re-elected next fall.

definition_en: characteristic of an owner of property; constituting property
example_sentence: To take a proprietary interest in something is to act as though you own it.

definition_en: properness; good manners
example_sentence: The old lady viewed the little girl's failure to curtsy as a flagrant breach of propriety.

definition_en: dull; unimaginative; like prose (as opposed to poetry)
example_sentence: Her description of the battle was so prosaic that it was hard for her listeners to believe that any of the soldiers had even been wounded.

definition_en: to outlaw; to prohibit
example_sentence: Spitting on the sidewalk and shooting at road signs were both proscribed activities under the new administration.

definition_en: to convert (someone) from one religion or doctrine to another; to recruit converts to a religion or doctrine
example_sentence: The former Methodist had been proselytized by a Lutheran deacon.

definition_en: the leading character in a novel, play, or other work; a leader or champion
example_sentence: Martin Luther King Jr. was a protagonist in the long and continuing struggle for racial equality.

definition_en: to prolong
example_sentence: The trial was so protracted that one of the jurors died of old age.

definition_en: preparing for the future; providing for the future; frugal
example_sentence: We were provident with our limited food supplies, knowing that the winter ahead would be long and cold.

definition_en: limited in outlook to one's own small corner of the world; narrow
example_sentence: The farmers were provincial; they had no opinions about anything but the price of corn.

definition_en: conditional; temporary; tentative
example_sentence: Louis had been accepted as a provisional member of the club. He wouldn't become a permanent member until the other members had had a chance to see what he was really like.

definition_en: nearness
example_sentence: In a big city, one is almost always in the proximity of a restaurant.

definition_en: careful; having foresight
example_sentence: Joe is a prudent money manager.

definition_en: rumored; claimed
example_sentence: The heiress is purported to have been kidnapped by adventurers and buried in a concrete vault.

definition_en: commonly accepted; supposed; reputed
example_sentence: The putative reason for placing the monument downtown is that nobody had wanted it uptown.

definition_en: to modify or restrict
example_sentence: Susan qualified her praise of Judith by saying that her kind words applied only to Judith's skillful cooking and not to her abhorrent personality.

definition_en: having to do with the quality or qualities of something (as opposed to the quantity)
example_sentence: The difference between the two restaurants was quantitative rather than qualitative.

definition_en: complaining; grumbling; whining
example_sentence: The exasperated mother finally managed to hush her querulous child.

definition_en: romantic or idealistic to a foolish or impractical degree
example_sentence: For many years Mr. Morris had led a quixotic effort to repeal the federal income tax.

definition_en: a consequence; a branching out
example_sentence: The professor found a solution to the problem, but there are many ramifications. Some experts are afraid that she has created more problems than she has solved.

definition_en: bitter, long-lasting ill will or resentment
example_sentence: The mutual rancor felt by the two nations eventually led to war.

definition_en: greedy; plundering; avaricious
example_sentence: Wall Street investment bankers are often accused of being rapacious, but they claim they are performing a valuable economic function.

definition_en: to criticize sharply
example_sentence: We trembled as Mr. Solomon rebuked us for flipping over his car and taking off the tires.

definition_en: to contradict; to argue in opposition to; to prove to be false
example_sentence: They all thought I was crazy, but none of them could rebut my argument.

definition_en: stubbornly defiant of authority or control; disobedient
example_sentence: The recalcitrant cancer continued to spread through the patient's body despite every therapy and treatment the doctors tried.

definition_en: to publicly take back and deny (something previously said or believed); to openly confess error
example_sentence: The chagrined scientist recanted his theory that mice originated on the moon.

definition_en: mutual; shared; interchangeable
example_sentence: The Rochester Club had a reciprocal arrangement with the Duluth Club.

definition_en: hermitlike; withdrawn from society
example_sentence: The crazy millionaire led a reclusive existence, shutting herself up in her labyrinthine mansion and never setting foot in the outside world.

definition_en: hard to understand; over one's head
example_sentence: The philosopher's thesis was so recondite that I couldn't get past the first two sentences.

definition_en: a bitter counteraccusation, or the act of making a bitter counteraccusation
example_sentence: Melissa was full of recrimination. When I accused her of stealing my pen, she angrily accused me of being careless, evil, and stupid.

definition_en: fragrant
example_sentence: The air in autumn is redolent of wood smoke and fallen leaves.

definition_en: unnecessarily repetitive; excessive; excessively wordy
example_sentence: Shawn's article was redundant-he kept saying the same thing over and over again.

definition_en: to prove to be false; to disprove
example_sentence: His expensive suit and imported shoes clearly refuted his claim that he was poor.

definition_en: to say again; to repeat
example_sentence: The candidate had reiterated his position so many times on the campaign trail that he sometimes even muttered it in his sleep.

definition_en: to banish; to send away
example_sentence: The most junior of the junior executives was relegated to a tiny, windowless office that had once been a broom closet.

definition_en: continuous; unstoppable
example_sentence: The insatiable rabbit was relentless; it ate and ate until nothing was left in the botanical garden.

definition_en: to release or let go of; to surrender; to stop doing
example_sentence: The retiring president relinquished control of the company only with the greatest reluctance.

definition_en: to argue against; to protest; to raise objections
example_sentence: My boss remonstrated with me for telling all the secretaries they could take off the rest of the week.

definition_en: a rebirth or revival
example_sentence: The declining neighborhood underwent a renaissance when group of investors bought several crumbling tenements and turned them into attractive apartment buildings.

definition_en: to give up formally or resign; to disown; to have nothing to do with anymore
example_sentence: Despite the pleadings and protestations of her parents, Deborah refused to renounce her love for the leader of the motorcycle gang.

definition_en: paying back; making amends; compensation
example_sentence: The defeated country demanded reparations for the destruction it had suffered at the hands of the victorious army.

definition_en: a consequence; an indirect effect
example_sentence: One repercussion of the new tax law was that accountants found themselves with a lot of new business.

definition_en: to fill again; to resupply; to restore
example_sentence: The manager of the hardware store needed to replenish his stock; quite a few of the shelves were empty.

definition_en: completely filled; abounding
example_sentence: The once-polluted stream was now replete with fish of every description.

definition_en: worthy of severe blame or censure
example_sentence: Malcolm's manners were reprehensible: he ate his soup by drinking it from his empty wineglass...

definition_en: a military action undertaken in revenge for another; an act of taking "an eye for an eye"
example_sentence: The raid on the Iranian oil-drilling platform was a reprisal for the Iranians' earlier attack on the American tanker.

definition_en: to scold, usually in disappointment; to blame; to disgrace
example_sentence: The police officer reproached me for leaving my car parked overnight in a no-standing zone.

definition_en: to criticize mildly
example_sentence: Aunt May reproved us for eating too much, but we could tell she was actually thrilled that we had enjoyed the meal.

definition_en: to reject; to renounce; to disown; to have nothing to do with
example_sentence: Hoping to receive a lighter sentence, the convicted gangster repudiated his former connection with the mob.

definition_en: required; necessary
example_sentence: Howard bought a hunting rifle and the requisite ammunition.

definition_en: determined; firm; unwavering
example_sentence: The other team was strong, but our players were resolute. They kept pushing and shoving until, in the final moments, they won the roller-derby tournament.

definition_en: a period of rest or relief
example_sentence: We worked without respite from five in the morning until five in the afternoon.

definition_en: quiet; restrained; reluctant to speak, especially about oneself
example_sentence: Luther's natural reticence made him an ideal speaker: his speeches never lasted more than a few minutes.

definition_en: to respect highly; to honor
example_sentence: Einstein was a preeminent scientist who was revered by everyone, even his rivals.

definition_en: the art of formal speaking or writing; inflated discourse
definition_ko: 형식적인 말이나 글의 기술, 수사학, 과장된 담화
example_sentence: A political candidate's speech that was long on drama and promises but short on genuine substance might be dismissed as 'mere rhetoric.'

definition_en: strict; harsh; severe
example_sentence: Our exercise program was rigorous but effective; after just a few months, our eighteen minutes of daily exercise had begun to pay off.

definition_en: strong and healthy; vigorous
example_sentence: The ninety-year-old woman was still robust. Every morning she ran several miles down to the ocean and jumped in.

definition_en: a criminally dishonest person; a scoundrel
example_sentence: Huckleberry Finn is a bit of a rogue; while his actions are technically criminal, he performs them with noble intentions and a humorous spirit.

definition_en: basic; crude; unformed or undeveloped
example_sentence: The boy who had lived with wolves for fifteen years lacked even the most rudimentary social skills.

definition_en: to contemplate; to ponder; to mull over
example_sentence: The teacher's comment about the causes of weather set me to ruminating about what a nice day it was and to wishing that I were outside.

definition_en: rural; lacking urban comforts or sophistication; primitive
definition_ko: 시골의, 도시적인 편안함이나 세련됨이 없는, 원시적인
example_sentence: Life in the log cabin was too rustic for Leah; she missed hot showers, electricity, and ice.

definition_en: sweet; excessively or disgustingly sweet
example_sentence: The love story was so saccharine that I vowed never to see another sappy, predictable movie again.

definition_en: a violation of something sacred; blasphemy
example_sentence: The minister committed the sacrilege of delivering his sermon while wearing his golf shoes.

definition_en: sacred; held to be inviolable
example_sentence: Mr. Peters's lunchtime trip to his neighborhood bar was sacrosanct; he would no sooner skip it than he would skip his mother's funeral.

definition_en: discerning; shrewd; keen in judgment; wise
definition_ko: 통찰력 있는, 기민한, 판단력이 예리한, 현명한
example_sentence: Edgar's decision to move the chickens into the barn turned out to be sagacious; about an hour later, the hailstorm hit.

definition_en: sticking out; conspicuous; leaping
example_sentence: Ursula had a number of salient features including, primarily, her nose, which stuck out so far that she was constantly in danger of slamming it in doors and windows.

definition_en: healthful; remedial; curative
example_sentence: Lowered blood pressure is among the salutary effects of exercise.

definition_en: pretending to be devout; affecting religious feeling
example_sentence: The sanctimonious old bore pretended to be deeply offended when Lucius whispered a mild swear word after dropping the hammer on his bare foot.

definition_en: cheerful; optimistic; hopeful
example_sentence: Miguel was sanguine about his chances of winning the Nobel Peace Prize, even though, as an eighth grader, he hadn't yet done anything to deserve it.

definition_en: mocking; scornful
example_sentence: Isabella's weak attempts at humor were met by nothing but a few scattered pockets of sardonic laughter.

definition_en: to sparkle, either literally or figuratively
definition_ko: 문자 그대로나 비유적으로 번쩍이다, 재치가 번뜩이다
example_sentence: Stefan was a quiet drudge at home, but at a party he could be absolutely scintillating, tossing off witty remarks and charming everyone in the room.

definition_en: strict; careful; hesitant for ethical reasons
definition_ko: 엄격한, 꼼꼼한, 윤리적인 이유로 망설이는, 양심적인
example_sentence: Leela was scrupulous in keeping her accounts; she knew where every penny came from and where every penny went.

definition_en: to examine very carefully
example_sentence: The rocket scientists scrutinized thousands of pages of computer printouts, looking for a clue to why the rocket had exploded.

definition_en: having nothing to do with religion or spiritual concerns
definition_ko: 종교나 영적인 문제와 관련이 없는, 세속적인
example_sentence: The group home had several nuns on its staff, but it was an entirely secular operation, run by the city, not the church.

definition_en: treason; the incitement of public disorder or rebellion
example_sentence: The political group was charged with sedition because it had advocated burning the capital to the ground.

definition_en: to separate
example_sentence: Rico kept his prize-winning poodle, Fluffy, segregated from his other two dogs, which were mixed breeds.

definition_en: having to do with the senses or sensation
example_sentence: Babies enjoy bright colors, moving objects, pleasant sounds, and other forms of sensory stimulation.

definition_en: able to perceive by the senses; conscious
definition_ko: 감각을 통해 인지할 수 있는, 의식이 있는
example_sentence: Human beings are sentient. Rocks are not.

definition_en: to set or keep apart
example_sentence: Juries are sometimes sequestered during trials to prevent them from talking to people or reading newspapers.

definition_en: accidental good fortune; discovering good things without looking for them
definition_ko: 우연한 행운, 찾지 않던 좋은 것을 발견하는 것
example_sentence: It was serendipity rather than genius that led the archaeologist to his breathtaking discovery of the ancient civilization.

definition_en: submissive and subservient; like a servant
example_sentence: Cat lovers sometimes say that dogs are too servile because they follow their owners everywhere and slobber all over them at every opportunity.

definition_en: unique; superior; exceptional; strange
example_sentence: Darren had the singular ability to stand on one big toe for several hours at a time.

definition_en: evil, wicked; foreshadowing evil, trouble, or wickedness
example_sentence: The house on the hill is pretty by day, but at night it casts sinister shadows and emits frightening moans.

definition_en: to speak badly about someone publicly; to defame; to spread malicious rumor
definition_ko: 공개적으로 누군가를 나쁘게 말하다, 명예를 훼손하다, 악의적인 소문을 퍼뜨리다
example_sentence: Jonathan slandered Mr. Perriwinkle by telling everyone in school that the principal was a thief.

definition_en: laziness; sluggishness
example_sentence: Yusuke's weekends were devoted to sloth. He never arose before noon and seldom left the house before Monday morning.

definition_en: the state of being sober; seriousness
example_sentence: Sobriety was such an unfamiliar condition that the reforming alcoholic didn't recognize it at first.

definition_en: eager and attentive, often to the point of hovering; anxiously caring or attentive
example_sentence: When the sick movie star sneezed, half-a-dozen solicitous nurses came rushing into her hospital room.

definition_en: not broke or bankrupt; able to pay one's bills
example_sentence: Jerry didn't hope to become a millionaire; all he wanted to do was remain solvent.

definition_en: sleep inducing; boring; sleepy
example_sentence: Sam's soporific address was acknowledged not by applause but by a chorus of snores.

definition_en: vile; filthy; squalid
example_sentence: The conspirators plotted their sordid schemes at a series of secret meetings in an abandoned warehouse.

definition_en: to bring forth; to produce a large number
example_sentence: A best-selling book or blockbuster movie will spawn dozens of imitators.

definition_en: deceptively plausible or attractive
example_sentence: The charlatan's specious theories about curing baldness with used tea bags charmed the studio audience but did not convince the experts.

definition_en: stopping and starting; scattered; occurring in bursts every once in a while
example_sentence: Kyle's attention to his schoolwork was sporadic at best; he tended to lose his concentration after a few minutes of effort.

definition_en: false; fake
example_sentence: The political candidate attributed his loss to numerous spurious rumors that had hounded him throughout his campaign.

definition_en: filth; wretched, degraded, or repulsive living conditions
example_sentence: If people live in squalor for too long, the ruling elite can count on an insurgency.

definition_en: to waste
example_sentence: Jerry failed to husband his inheritance; instead, he squandered it on trips to Las Vegas.

definition_en: motionlessness; inactivity
example_sentence: The company grew quickly for several years; then it fell into stagnation.

definition_en: stationary; not changing or moving
example_sentence: Sales of the new book soared for a few weeks then became static.

definition_en: firmly committed; firmly in favor of; steadfast
example_sentence: A staunch supporter of tax reform would be someone who firmly believes in tax reform.

definition_en: loyal; faithful
example_sentence: Steadfast love is love that never wavers. To be steadfast in a relationship is to be faithfully committed.

definition_en: to brand with disgrace; to set a mark of disgrace upon
example_sentence: Steve once went into the girls' bathroom by accident, and this mistake stigmatized him for the rest of his high school career.

definition_en: to require something as part of an agreement
example_sentence: You are well-advised to stipulate the maximum amount you will pay in any car-repair contract.

definition_en: indifferent (at least outwardly) to pleasure or pain, to joy or grief, to fortune or misfortune
definition_ko: 쾌락이나 고통, 기쁨이나 슬픔, 행운이나 불행에 무관심한, 금욕주의적인
example_sentence: Nina was stoic about the death of her canary; she went about her business as though nothing sad had happened.

definition_en: a layer; a level
example_sentence: The middle class is one stratum of society.

definition_en: a restriction; a limitation; a negative criticism
example_sentence: Despite the strictures of apartment living, we enjoyed the eight years we spent in New York City.

definition_en: bitter conflict; discord; a struggle or clash
example_sentence: Marital strife often leads to divorce.

definition_en: strict; restrictive
example_sentence: The restaurant's stringent dress code required male diners to wear a suit coat and tie or they had to leave.

definition_en: to thwart; to get in the way of; to hinder
example_sentence: In my effort to make a name for myself in the company, I was stymied by my boss, who always managed to take credit for all the good things I did.

definition_en: to subdue and dominate; to enslave
example_sentence: The tyrant subjugated all the peasants living in the kingdom; once free, they were now forced to do her bidding.

definition_en: awesome; extremely exalted; lofty; majestic
definition_ko: 장엄한, 매우 숭고한, 숭고한, 위엄 있는
example_sentence: Theodore was a sublime thinker; after pondering even a difficult problem for just a few minutes, he would invariably arrive at a concise and elegant solution.

definition_en: lower in importance, position, or rank; secondary
definition_ko: 중요도, 지위, 계급이 더 낮은, 부차적인
example_sentence: A vice president is subordinate to a president.

definition_en: having substance; real; essential; solid; substantial
example_sentence: The differences between the two theories were not substantive; in fact, the two theories said the same thing with different words.

definition_en: not obvious; able to make fine distinctions; ingenious; crafty
definition_ko: 명백하지 않은, 미묘한 차이를 구분할 수 있는, 교묘한, 영리한
example_sentence: Jim's subtle mind enables him to see past problems that confuse the rest of us.

definition_en: corrupting; overthrowing; undermining; insurgent
example_sentence: The political group destroyed the Pentagon's computer files, hijacked Air Force One, and engaged in various other subversive activities.

definition_en: brief and to the point; concise
example_sentence: Aaron's succinct explanation of why the moon doesn't fall out of the sky and crash into the earth quickly satisfied even the most skeptical of the seventh graders.

definition_en: to yield or submit; to die
example_sentence: The Martians in The War of the Worlds survived every military weapon known to man but succumbed to the common cold.

definition_en: haughty; patronizing
example_sentence: The supercilious Rolls-Royce salesman treated us like peasants until we opened our suitcase full of one-hundred-dollar bills.

definition_en: on the surface only; shallow; not thorough
example_sentence: Tom had indeed been shot, but the wound was superficial the bullet had merely creased the tip of his nose.

definition_en: extra; unnecessary; redundant
example_sentence: Andrew's attempt to repair the light bulb was superfluous because the light bulb had already been repaired.

definition_en: excess; an excessive amount; excess or over-indulgence in eating or drinking
example_sentence: Thanksgiving meals are usually a surfeit for everyone involved.

definition_en: sneaky; secret
example_sentence: The babysitter made herself a surreptitious meal of lobster as soon as Mr. and Mrs. Robinson had driven away.

definition_en: substitute
example_sentence: A kind parent offered to go to prison as a surrogate for his son, who had been convicted of extortion.

definition_en: one who sucks up to others
example_sentence: The French class seemed to be full of sycophants; the students were always bringing apples to the teacher and telling her how nice she looked.

definition_en: the combining of parts to form a whole
definition_ko: 전체를 형성하기 위해 부분을 결합하는 것, 종합
example_sentence: A hot fudge sundae is the perfect synthesis of hot fudge and vanilla ice cream.

definition_en: implied; not spoken

definition_en: untalkative by nature

definition_en: only superficially related to the matter at hand; not especially relevant; peripheral

definition_en: equivalent to

definition_en: boldness; recklessness; audacity

definition_en: mild; moderate; restrained

definition_en: a shared principle or belief

definition_en: experimental; temporary; uncertain

definition_en: using no unnecessary words; succinct

definition_en: the study of God or religion

definition_en: a prolonged, bitter speech

definition_en: sluggishness; inactivity; apathy

definition_en: to go beyond or above; to surpass

definition_en: oily, both literally and figuratively; insincere

definition_en: unceasing; unabated; relentless

definition_ko: 매수되기 쉬운, 돈을 위해 뭐든 하는, 부패한

confusions.difference: venal=부패한, 매수하기 쉬운/venial=가벼운, 용서받을 수 있는

definition_en: to revere; to treat as something holy, especially because of great age

music written for and performed by small ensembles of players

the distance that light travels in a year, or approximately 5,878,000,000,000 miles

definition_en: an enormous cloud of dust and gas in outer space

definition_en: the process whereby green plants transform energy from the sun into food
